stem: In a circle, a central angle subtending arc \( AB \) measures \( [[c]]^{\circ} \). What is the measure, in degrees, of an inscribed angle that subtends the same arc \( AB \)?
solution: An inscribed angle is half the central angle that subtends the same arc. Here the central angle is \( [[c]]^{\circ} \), so the inscribed angle measures \( \frac{[[c]]}{2} = [[answer]] \) degrees.
distractor_rationales: This repeats the central angle, forgetting that the inscribed angle is half of it.
This doubles the central angle instead of halving it (inverting the relationship).
This takes the supplement of the central angle rather than half of it.
This is the complement of the correct inscribed angle.
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.

stem: Triangle \( ABC \) is inscribed in a circle so that side \( AC \) is a diameter of the circle. If \( \angle A = [[acute]]^{\circ} \), what is the measure, in degrees, of \( \angle B \)?
solution: By Thales' theorem, an angle inscribed in a semicircle (i.e., subtending a diameter) is always a right angle. Since \( AC \) is a diameter, \( \angle B = 90^{\circ} \) regardless of the value of \( \angle A \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the triangle's angles as summing with \( \angle B \) unknown from a linear equation, but ignores that \( \angle B \) is fixed at \( 90^{\circ} \) by Thales' theorem.
This subtracts \( \angle A \) from \( 90^{\circ} \) rather than recognizing \( \angle B \) itself is the fixed right angle.
This just repeats \( \angle A \) instead of identifying the right angle at \( B \).
This doubles \( \angle A \), confusing it with an inscribed-central angle relationship.
This is half of the correct right angle.
This is a full straight angle, not the inscribed right angle.

stem: Quadrilateral \( ABCD \) is inscribed in a circle (a cyclic quadrilateral). If \( \angle A = [[angA]]^{\circ} \), what is the measure, in degrees, of the opposite angle \( \angle C \)?
solution: In a cyclic quadrilateral, opposite angles are supplementary. So \( \angle C = 180^{\circ} - \angle A = 180 - [[angA]] = [[answer]] \) degrees.
distractor_rationales: This treats opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral as equal instead of supplementary.
This subtracts from \( 360^{\circ} \) instead of \( 180^{\circ} \), as if all four angles summed differently.
This applies an inscribed-angle-style halving that doesn't belong to this relationship.
This halves \( \angle A \) instead of finding its supplement.
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.

stem: An inscribed angle in a circle subtends arc \( XY \) and measures \( [[insc]]^{\circ} \). What is the measure, in degrees, of arc \( XY \) (the arc not containing the inscribed angle's vertex)?
solution: The measure of an arc equals twice the inscribed angle that subtends it. So arc \( XY = 2\cdot[[insc]] = [[answer]] \) degrees.
distractor_rationales: This confuses the arc measure with the inscribed angle itself, forgetting to double it.
This halves the inscribed angle instead of doubling it, inverting the relationship.
This takes the supplement of the inscribed angle rather than doubling it.
This finds the REMAINING arc (the rest of the circle) instead of arc \( XY \) itself.
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.

stem: Two inscribed angles in a circle stand on the same arc, so they are equal. One measures \( ([[a]]x + [[b]])^{\circ} \) and the other measures \( ([[c]]x + [[d]])^{\circ} \). What is the measure, in degrees, of either angle?
solution: Since the two inscribed angles stand on the same arc, they are equal: \( [[a]]x+[[b]] = [[c]]x+[[d]] \). Solving gives \( x = \frac{[[d]]-[[b]]}{[[a]]-[[c]]} \), and substituting back gives an angle measure of \( [[answer]] \) degrees.
distractor_rationales: This reports just the value of \( x \) rather than substituting it back to find the angle measure.
This reports \( x \) itself, not the resulting angle.
This takes the supplement of the correct angle instead of the angle itself.
This simply adds the two constant terms instead of solving the equation for \( x \) first.
This is off from the correct value by five degrees.
This is off from the correct value by five degrees.
